title: Inpainting with Diffusion Models
concept: During sampling, the known (unmasked) pixels are kept fixed at each step while only the masked region is denoised, guided by the surrounding context.

exercises.prompt: You spill coffee on a printed photo, covering part of it. To fix it digitally, what's the smartest way to fill in the missing part?

options.text: Guess randomly with no reference to the rest of the photo
Use the visible parts of the photo as clues to fill in the missing spot
Delete the whole photo and start over

exercises.explain: Just like a restorer studies the surrounding image to fill in a damaged spot, AI uses the visible pixels as clues to rebuild the missing region!

cards.title: Fixing the Missing Piece
cards.body: Imagine a puzzle with one piece missing. You don't need to guess blindly — you can look at the picture around the hole to figure out what belongs there. Diffusion models do exactly this to 'inpaint' missing or masked parts of images!

cards.title: What is Inpainting?
cards.body: Inpainting means filling in a missing or masked part of an image. With diffusion models, during each denoising step, the pixels we already know (unmasked) are kept exactly as they are, while only the masked (missing) region gets denoised — guided by the known pixels around it.

cards.title: The Puzzle Restorer
cards.body: Think of an art restorer repainting a torn corner of an old painting. They never touch the parts that are already fine — they keep those exactly as they are. They only paint the torn section, constantly looking at the colors and shapes around it to make it blend in perfectly.

cards.title: Removing a Photobomber
cards.body: Say a stranger walks into your vacation photo. You mask out that person's pixels. The diffusion model keeps the beach, sky, and your friends exactly as they were, and only 'imagines' new pixels in the masked area — matching the sand and sky around it — so it looks like the photobomber was never there.

cards.title: Myth: The Whole Image Gets Redrawn
cards.body: Myth: Inpainting redraws the entire picture from scratch. Truth: Only the masked region changes! The known pixels are held fixed at every single denoising step, so the rest of your image stays perfectly untouched and identical to the original.

cards.title: Masked vs Unmasked Regions
cards.body: During each sampling step, the model treats the two regions very differently.

left.text: Kept fixed at every step — never changed, exactly as the original image

right.text: Actively denoised at every step — guided by the fixed pixels around it

exercises.prompt: During diffusion inpainting, what happens to the known (unmasked) pixels at each sampling step?

options.text: They are kept fixed and unchanged

exercises.explain: Correct! The unmasked pixels stay fixed at every step, acting as an anchor so only the masked region is generated.

exercises.prompt: True or False: In diffusion-based inpainting, the entire image is denoised from scratch, including parts we already know.
exercises.explain: False — only the masked (unknown) region is denoised. Known pixels are held fixed throughout the process.

exercises.sentence: During inpainting, the masked region is denoised while being ___ by the surrounding known pixels.
exercises.explain: The surrounding known pixels guide the denoising of the masked region, helping it blend naturally with the rest of the image.

exercises.prompt: Which of these best describes why keeping unmasked pixels fixed is important for inpainting?

options.text: It ensures the final image matches the original perfectly outside the masked area
It makes the model run faster with less memory
It allows the model to skip the masked region entirely

exercises.explain: Keeping unmasked pixels fixed guarantees the output is identical to the original image everywhere except the masked region, which gets newly generated content.

exercises.prompt: A photo editing app uses a diffusion model to remove an object from a picture. You mask the object's region. What should happen during the sampling process?

options.text: The whole image, including the background, gets redrawn from pure noise
Only the masked region is denoised at each step, while the rest of the image stays fixed exactly as it was, guiding the fill-in
The model deletes the masked area and leaves it blank
The model copies pixels from a random unrelated image into the mask

exercises.explain: This is the core idea of diffusion inpainting: known pixels stay fixed at every denoising step, and they guide the generation of new content only in the masked region — producing a seamless, realistic result.
